chunk_summary: Guidelines for diagnosing and managing microcytic anemia in children, focusing on iron trials, ferritin interpretation, and stepwise testing strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand when to initiate a diagnostic iron trial versus laboratory testing in microcytic anemia.
Learn how to interpret ferritin levels in the context of inflammation and infection.
Identify stepwise diagnostic approaches for microcytic anemia, including red flag scenarios.
Avoid common pitfalls in iron deficiency anemia diagnosis and treatment.
key_facts: A rise in hemoglobin (Hb) >1 g/dL after 4 weeks of oral iron therapy confirms iron deficiency in mild anemia.
In severe anemia, Hb improvement may be seen within 2 weeks if adherence is good.
Ferritin levels should be interpreted with inflammation markers like CRP or AGP, as ferritin rises in infection.
Stepwise testing starts with CBC and smear, followed by ferritin, and expands to stool occult blood, celiac screening, or hemoglobinopathy testing if red flags are present.
Avoid relying on a single low serum iron value for treatment decisions; use ferritin and clinical phenotype instead.
reference_quote: Ferritin reflects iron stores but rises in infection/inflammation; in children with recent or active inflammation, higher ferritin cutoffs are used to define deficiency than in apparently healthy children.
document_key_topics: Diagnostic iron trial in microcytic anemia
Ferritin interpretation in the context of inflammation
Stepwise diagnostic approach for pediatric anemia
Avoiding pitfalls in iron deficiency anemia management
